title: Japanese GP Reactions: Keisner's Dream, Ferrari's Humbling, and Nathanial's Heartbreak
meta: Published: 30 March 2026 | By Olly Bradshaw, Paddock Insider | F1CC News
caption: Robin Keisner celebrates his maiden victory with the Audi team at Suzuka.
body:
Robin Keisner won the Japanese Grand Prix.

Read that again.

Audi's first Formula 1 victory. A driver who, twelve months ago, was scrapping for 17th in a Kick Sauber. Suzuka, in the rain, with the championship leaders tripping over themselves.

The paddock is still struggling to process it.

Keisner himself, usually understated, was emotional. “We fought hard. It's been always my dream to stand on the podium, especially in Japan, my second home,” he said. “The team developed the car to a point where I feel really comfortable with it. The only thing I'm currently struggling with is the qualifying pace.”

He saved particular praise for the man who finished second. “I'm also proud to stand on the podium with my good friend Aygen. He really pushed me through the last bit.”

Friendship on the podium. How often do you see that…?

Audi mechanics were seen embracing in the garage long after the chequered flag. One, a veteran who had been with the team since the Sauber days, wiped away tears and said simply: “We never stopped believing. Robin never stopped believing. This is for everyone who thought we were just making up the numbers.”

Ferrari: Humbled

Randy Belacik didn't sugarcoat it.

“Japan humbled us. We accept that. Ferrari has been humbled before and Ferrari has always responded. The rain took nothing from this team that we cannot find again. Eetu showed us the way. Bahrain will be our answer.”

Strong words. But the facts are stark: a seventh place from pole, a strategy that backfired, and a second car still pointless and mired in penalty trouble.

Eetu Väisänen was briefer. “Strategy didn't pay off. There's always the next race…”

The ellipsis felt like it carried more weight than the words.

Williams: Joy and Pain

Jack TEF's post was a study in contrasts.

“Look. Today has certainly been a mixed one. Firstly, I am absolutely thrilled for Eren. I remember last year standing on the podium beside him here in Japan after his first win, and standing down there watching him on the podium again, makes me real proud.”

Then the shadow. Lucas Koopman's lap‑one crash, and the back injury that will sideline him. “Back pains aren't a joke in this sport, I fully back Koopman with his recovery, pardon my pun.”

And the call‑up. “As for his replacement, Hamish is a perfect fit. He is P2 in Formula 2 and has shown already he is capable. I apologise to him again that his F2 title hopes may be significantly hindered with 3 weekends away, but he knows duty calls.”

Hamish Porter's F2 campaign on pause. A Williams debut awaiting. That's a story that will run and run.

Nathanial: The Inevitability of Disaster

Joshua Nathanial's voice was steady, but the words were hollow.

“Hard to put that into words to be honest — I did everything to put myself into a position to win this race and I find myself standing in the press pen once again before the race ends. It just seems like an inevitability whenever I'm in a good position at this point, so I don't know what more I can do.”

He was hit from behind by Maikel Zwart, a collision that wiped out both cars. Nathanial still has zero points in 2026.

“Maikel came over and apologised which I respect, but it doesn't change us leaving with a destroyed car and 25 less points. The silver lining is the race pace was excellent up to that stage, so we go to Bahrain looking to hit back — I won't be pulling any punches, that's for sure.”

Zero points after four rounds. The best race of his season, gone in a moment. How long before that smile cracks…?

Springfield: Waiting for Help That Never Came

Sarah Springfield ran as high as second and finished fifth, a result that on paper looks strong. But the strategy left her frustrated.

“We were staying out hoping for a VSC or a Safety Car but it just came too late for us to benefit from. Great day regardless with the P5, but we still have work to do to stop making it seem like I'm the only one who's pulling their weight.”

That last sentence will echo around the Aston Martin garage. Springfield has scored 24 of the team's 24 points this season.

Haas: The Long Road

Haas Team Principal Jack Stone summed up a difficult weekend with his usual honesty.

“First of all it's obviously not the debut weekend we wanted here at Suzuka. The conditions were incredibly challenging all weekend, and with the rain constantly changing the grip levels, it made every session difficult to manage. In the race Riley drove well and kept fighting despite the conditions, but missing out on the points after such a demanding afternoon is tough to take. George was very unlucky to be taken out. There was really nothing he could do in that situation.”

Riley Swansson added his own perspective. “Well I mean I felt really fast coming through those corners and the straights, but all that time I got was lost on the corner exits. But anyway, we did what we could have. Hopefully we improve next time out, thank you.”

A Haas mechanic, leaning against a tyre rack in the garage, was more direct. “We've got pace, we just can't get it out of the corners. It's like the car has a secret it's not telling us. But we'll find it. We always do.”

The Bigger Picture

Väisänen still leads the championship with 64 points. Keisner is now second on 41. Aygen 34, Vandesteene 33, Kevu 28. The field is compressing.

Ferrari were humbled. Mercedes lost a probable win through a strategy gamble and a driver's misjudgment. Williams lost a driver but gained a podium and a new face. Audi gained a first victory and a new belief.

Suzuka always reshuffles the order. This time, it might have reshuffled it for good.

Watch this space.
